'''Hugh Ferguson''' (2 March 1895 – 8 January 1930) was a Scottish professional [[Association football|footballer]]. Born in [[Motherwell]], he played for [[Parkhead F.C.|Parkhead]] at [[Scottish Junior Football Association|junior level]] as an amateur and was one of the most sought-after young players in Scotland before signing for his hometown club, [[Motherwell F.C.]], to begin his professional career. He established himself as a consistent scorer playing as a [[Forward (association football)#Centre forward|centre forward]], finishing as the top goalscorer in the [[Scottish Football League]] on three occasions between 1918 and 1921. His 284 league goals remains a [[List of Motherwell F.C. records and statistics|record]] at the club and, by 1925, he was the highest-scoring player in the history of the Scottish League.

In 1925, Ferguson moved to Welsh side [[Cardiff City F.C.|Cardiff City]] for £5,000 ({{Inflation|UK|5000|1925|fmt=eq|cursign=£|r=-4}}) and continued his scoring exploits. He was the club's top goalscorer for four consecutive seasons and scored the winning goal in the [[1927 FA Cup Final]] during a 1–0 victory over [[Arsenal F.C.|Arsenal]]. He also scored in the [[1927 FA Charity Shield]], during a 2–1 victory over amateur side [[Corinthians F.C.|Corinthians]]. Both results made Cardiff the only non-English team to have won the [[FA Cup]] or the [[FA Charity Shield]]. Despite his prolific scoring record, finishing his career with a goal average of 0.855 per game, he was never selected to play for [[Scotland national football team|Scotland]], but did represent a [[Scottish League XI]] on three occasions.

Ferguson returned to Scotland with [[Dundee F.C.|Dundee]] in 1929, but struggled to reproduce his goalscoring form. Six months after his arrival, he had lost his place on the team.  He committed suicide on 8 January 1930 at the age of 34. He is one out of only seven men in the history of the English and Scottish Football Leagues to have scored 350 league goals.

==Career==
===Early career===
[[File:Ferguson Parkhead 1916.jpg|thumb|Ferguson (jumping, right) heads a ball while playing for [[Parkhead F.C.|Parkhead Juniors]] in the 1916 [[Scottish Junior Cup]] final.|alt=A black and white image of a footballer jumping to head a ball.]]
Ferguson started his career with local youth sides in his hometown of [[Motherwell]] and represented the [[Dalziel High School|Dalziel School]]'s team as a [[Midfield|half back]]. He moved on to the Motherwell branch of the [[The Boys' Brigade in the United Kingdom and Republic of Ireland|Boys' Brigade]] and later Motherwell Hearts as an [[Forward (association football)|outside forward]], helping the club reach the Scottish Juvenile Cup final. He joined [[Glasgow]]-based [[Parkhead F.C.|Parkhead]] in 1914,<ref name="new">{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000728/19170524/126/0006 |url-access=subscription |title=The League's New Centre |newspaper=Daily Record |page=6 |date=26 May 1917 |access-date=18 January 2021 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref><ref name="mtfee">{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000473/19251106/134/0008 |url-access=subscription |title=£5,000 For Ferguson |newspaper=Motherwell Times |page=8 |date=6 November 1925 |access-date=5 January 2020 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> when he switched to playing as a [[Forward (association football)#Centre forward|centre forward]]. He appeared for the side in their victorious 1914–15 [[Scottish Junior Cup]] final over [[Port Glasgow Athletic Juniors F.C.|Port Glasgow Athletic Juniors]]. Ferguson opened the scoring after 20 minutes by hitting a first-time shot past the opposition goalkeeper, which ''[[The Sunday Post]]'' described as "a capital goal". Parkhead went on to win the match 2–0.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000565/19150523/153/0010 |url-access=subscription |title=Record Crowd Views Scottish Junior Final |newspaper=The Sunday Post |page=10 |date=23 May 1915 |access-date=3 January 2020 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> At the end of his first season with Parkhead, he was chosen in a [[Glasgow Junior League]] XI against a Rest of Scotland XI and scored three of his side's goals in a 5–1 victory. The ''Midlothian Journal'' remarked that Ferguson was "in a class by himself".<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0002721/19150702/069/0006 |url-access=subscription |title=Football Notes |newspaper=Midlothian Journal |page=6 |date=2 July 1915 |access-date=16 January 2021 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> He entered into contract talks with [[Manchester City F.C.|Manchester City]] and was on the verge of signing for the club when the First World War escalated. With the suspension of football in England, the move was cancelled.<ref name="mc">{{Cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000473/19190124/064/0005 |url-access=subscription |title=Will Hugh Ferguson Go South? |newspaper=Motherwell Times |page=5 |date=24 January 1919 |access-date=4 January 2020 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref>

Ahead of the [[1915–16 in Scottish football|1915–16 season]], with Scottish football continuing during the First World War, Ferguson held talks with his hometown side [[Motherwell F.C.|Motherwell]] and missed the start of Parkhead's opening game of the campaign after extensive discussions with Motherwell manager [[John Hunter (footballer, born 1878)|John Hunter]] ran late.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000563/19150825/095/0005 |url-access=subscription |title=This Week's League Fixtures in Scotland |newspaper=Dundee Evening Telegraph |page=5 |date=25 August 1915 |access-date=16 January 2021 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> He remained with Parkhead and by the mid-point of the campaign, Ferguson was one of the most coveted young players in the country,<ref name="gos">{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0003159/19160128/056/0004 |url-access=subscription |title=Gossip on Sport |newspaper=Sporting Chronicle |page=4 |date=28 January 1916 |access-date=3 January 2020 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> having scored more than 30 goals in the first four months of the season,<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000728/19151231/087/0006 |url-access=subscription |title=Field and Pavilion |newspaper=Daily Record |page=6 |date=31 December 1915 |access-date=16 January 2021 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> and gaining the notice of several clubs.<ref name="gos"/> He turned down a second offer to join Motherwell the same month and was due to turn out for [[Rangers F.C.|Rangers]] in a benefit match a month later, but withdrew after sustaining an injury while playing for Parkhead in the Scottish Junior Cup, a tie in which he scored five times.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000728/19151210/108/0006 |url-access=subscription |title=Field and Pavilion |newspaper=Daily Record |page=6 |date=10 December 1915 |access-date=16 January 2021 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000728/19160105/119/0006 |url-access=subscription |title=Field and Pavilion |newspaper=Daily Record |page=6 |date=5 January 1916 |access-date=16 January 2021 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> He was chosen to represent the Glasgow Junior League XI against a team from the Rest of Scotland for the second time in February 1916,<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0001579/19160202/086/0003 |url-access=subscription |title=Football |newspaper=Kirkintilloch Herald |page=3 |date=2 February 1916 |access-date=3 January 2020 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> and scored one of his side's goals in a 5–2 victory.<ref name="is">{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000728/19160306/120/0006 |url-access=subscription |title=Juniors Help |newspaper=Daily Record |page=6 |date=6 March 1916 |access-date=3 January 2020 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> The match served as a trial for the selection of a Scottish Junior side to take on their [[Northern Ireland national Junior football team|Irish counterparts]] in March, and Ferguson was duly selected. Against the Irish side, he was [[Marking (association football)|marked]] closely and missed several chances as his frustration over his treatment grew. He did win a [[Penalty kick (association football)|penalty]] in the second half that he converted to seal a 2–0 win for Scotland.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000471/19160318/166/0006 |url-access=subscription |title=Junior Jottings |newspaper=Hamilton Advertiser |page=6 |date=18 March 1916 |access-date=3 January 2020 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref>

[[Syd Puddefoot]] recalled Ferguson playing against [[Glasgow United F.C.|Shettleston]] in a Scottish Junior Cup match that had been delayed and risked the forward missing an appointment; Ferguson proceeded to score eight times for Parkhead before feigning injury to leave the game early with the result no longer in doubt. Parkhead went on to win the game 11–1.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000728/19160214/133/0006 |url-access=subscription |title=Petershill Again |newspaper=Daily Record |page=6 |date=14 February 1916 |access-date=16 January 2021 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0001034/19251108/262/0021 |url-access=subscription |title=Hugh Ferguson Will Be Big Success |newspaper=Reynolds' Newspaper |last=Puddefoot |first=Sydney |page=21 |date=8 November 1925 |access-date=5 January 2020 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> Ferguson also helped Parkhead reach a second consecutive Scottish Junior Cup final in 1916, but his side were defeated 2–0 by [[Petershill F.C.|Petershill]].<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000565/19160528/070/0014 |title=Petershill Worthy Winners |newspaper=Sunday Post |page=14 |date=28 May 1916 |access-date=3 January 2020 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref>

After the 1916 final, many of Parkhead's squad were signed by professional clubs.<ref name="sc">{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0003159/19160804/048/0004 |url-access=subscription |title=Fishing for Juniors |newspaper=Sporting Chronicle |page=4 |date=4 August 1916 |access-date=3 January 2020 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> Ferguson, who had scored more than 70 goals in his last year with the side,<ref name="mwdebut">{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000728/19160821/109/0005 |url-access=subscription |title=League Football in Full Swing |newspaper=Daily Record |page=5 |date=21 August 1916 |access-date=3 January 2020 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> retained his amateur status longer than his teammates as he hoped for an offer from a [[Lancashire]]-based side in the [[English Football League|Football League]]. The ''[[Sporting Chronicle]]'' described Ferguson as "the most debated junior player of his time", some regarding him as a "mere goalscorer", but added that "there is not a senior club in Scotland who would not give £10, many times multiplied, to get Ferguson".<ref name="sc"/> Ferguson returned to Parkhead for the start of the 1916–17 season and scored twice for the club in a 3–1 victory over [[Strathclyde F.C.|Strathclyde]].<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000728/19160814/114/0005 |url-access=subscription |title=Clever Parkhead Forwards |newspaper=Daily Record |page=5 |date=14 August 1916 |access-date=3 January 2020 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref>

===Motherwell===
====Debut season and first top scorer award====
[[File:Ferguson Motherwell 1916.jpg|thumb|Ferguson (left) advances on goal during his senior debut for [[Motherwell F.C.|Motherwell]] in August 1916.|alt=A footballer runs towards a goalkeeper during a match.]]
Ferguson came close to signing for [[Celtic F.C.|Celtic]],<ref name="mc"/> but eventually joined his hometown side Motherwell for the start of the [[1916–17 Scottish Football League|1916–17 Scottish Football League season]]. Following the collapse of his move to Manchester, he had stated that Motherwell was "the only other club he would think about".<ref name="new"/> He made his professional debut for the club in a 2–2 draw with [[Raith Rovers F.C.|Raith Rovers]] on 19 August 1916, scoring both of his side's goals.<ref name="mwdebut"/> Two weeks later, he scored his first senior [[hat-trick]] during a 4–2 win over [[Dundee F.C.|Dundee]] and had a later strike disallowed for an earlier [[Fouls and misconduct (association football)|foul]].<ref name="md42">{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000473/19160908/026/0003 |url-access=subscription |title=Motherwell 4; Dundee 2 |newspaper=Motherwell Times |page=3 |date=8 September 1916 |access-date=3 January 2020 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000728/19160905/068/0005 |url-access=subscription |title=Field and Pavilion |newspaper=Daily Record |page=5 |date=5 September 1916 |access-date=19 January 2021 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> Motherwell had trailed 1–0 at half-time before Ferguson inspired a second-half comeback.<ref name="md42"/> The first half of his season was limited owing to minor injuries, but he went on to finish his first campaign with 25 league goals,<ref name="new"/><ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000728/19170421/145/0006 |url-access=subscription |title=Racing and Football |newspaper=Daily Record |page=6 |date=21 April 1917 |access-date=3 January 2020 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> including a second hat-trick against [[Dumbarton F.C.|Dumbarton]] in February 1917.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000728/19170212/102/0005 |url-access=subscription |title=Celtic in Clover at Greenock |newspaper=Daily Record |page=5 |date=12 February 1917 |access-date=18 January 2021 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> His tally was the fourth highest in the league and accounted for nearly half his side's goals during the campaign.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0001914/19170526/078/0008 |url-access=subscription |title=Men on the Mark |newspaper=Star Green 'Un |page=7 |date=26 May 1917 |access-date=18 January 2021 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000728/19170425/098/0006 |title=Field and Pavilion |newspaper=Daily Record |page=6 |date=25 April 1917 |access-date=3 January 2020 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> At the end of the season, he was selected in a Scottish League representative side for a benefit match against league champions Celtic;<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000728/19170427/127/0006 |url-access=subscription |title=For War Funds |newspaper=Daily Record |page=6 |date=27 April 1917 |access-date=3 January 2020 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref><ref name=sfl17>{{cite news |url=https://news.google.com/newspapers?id=xc1AAAAAIBAJ&pg=4053%2C4664689 |title=Notes on Sport. Football's Exit |newspaper=The Glasgow Herald |date=28 May 1917 |access-date=19 January 2021}}</ref> the ''[[Sheffield Star Green 'Un]]'' remarked that "few footballers have made their reputation as quickly".<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0001914/19170526/057/0005 |url-access=subscription |title=A Quick Rise to Fame |newspaper=Star Green' Un |page=5 |date=26 May 1917 |access-date=18 January 2021 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref>

In the opening game of the [[1917–18 in Scottish football|1917–18 season]], Ferguson opened his account for the new campaign by netting all four of Motherwell's goals during a 4–2 victory over [[Third Lanark A.C.|Third Lanark]].<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000540/19170820/305/0008 |url-access=subscription |title=Scottish Season Opened |newspaper=The Scotsman |page=8 |date=20 August 1917 |access-date=3 January 2020 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> During a league match against [[St. Mirren F.C.|St Mirren]] in October, he was knocked unconscious after being fouled in the opposition [[penalty area]]. The referee awarded a penalty for the foul, from which the winning goal was scored. Ferguson was stretchered off the pitch and did not play for another three weeks.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000728/19171015/115/0006 |url-access=subscription |title=Motherwell's Courage |newspaper=Daily Record |page=6 |date=15 October 1917 |access-date=18 January 2021 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000471/19171020/160/0007 |url-access=subscription |title=Mems |newspaper=Hamilton Advertiser |page=7 |date=20 October 1917 |access-date=18 January 2021 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000471/19171117/138/0006 |url-access=subscription |title=Mems |newspaper=Hamilton Advertiser |page=6 |date=17 November 1917 |access-date=18 January 2021 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> Further hat-tricks followed during the season; the first, against [[Ayr United F.C.|Ayr United]] in December,<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000540/19171203/139/0003 |url-access=subscription |title=Surprise Defeat of Kilmarnock |newspaper=The Scotsman |page=3 |date=3 December 1917 |access-date=3 January 2020 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> placed Ferguson as the highest-scoring player in Scotland at the end of 1917 with 22 goals.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0001914/19180112/011/0002 |url-access=subscription |title=McLean's Harvest |newspaper=Star Green 'Un |page=2 |date=12 January 1918 |access-date=18 January 2021 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> The second came a month later, against [[Queen's Park F.C.|Queen's Park]] in January 1918.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/BL/0000565/19180113/097/0011?browse=true |url-access=subscription |title=Ferguson's Three |newspaper=Sunday Post |page=11 |date=13 January 1918 |access-date=3 January 2020 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> He finished the season as the Scottish League's top goalscorer with 34, three ahead of Third Lanark's [[David McLean (footballer, born 1890)|David McLean]].<ref name="pw"/><ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0001914/19180518/008/0002 |url-access=subscription |title=Mainly About Players |newspaper=Star Green 'Un |page=2 |date=2 May 1918 |access-date=3 January 2020 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> His goals helped Motherwell record a fifth-placed finish and become the highest-scoring team in the league, Ferguson again scoring around half of his side's goals.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000471/19180420/167/0006 |url-access=subscription |title=Senior Jottings |newspaper=Hamilton Advertiser |page=6 |date=20 April 1918 |access-date=3 January 2020 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref>

====Further top five finishes====
Motherwell began the [[1918–19 in Scottish football|1918–19 season]] in poor form, scoring only twice in their opening five matches, one of which was claimed by Ferguson.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000471/19180921/149/0005 |url-access=subscription |title=Motherwell Echoes |newspaper=Hamilton Advertiser |page=5 |date=21 September 1918 |access-date=19 January 2021 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> His season was affected by illness after he contracted a throat infection which forced him to miss most of the final two months of 1918.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000728/19181127/097/0010 |url-access=subscription |title=An Airdrie Repeat |newspaper=Daily Record |page=10 |date=27 November 1918 |access-date=19 January 2021 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000728/19181219/084/0010 |url-access=subscription |title=Motherwell's Very Best |newspaper=Daily Record |page=10 |date=19 December 1918 |access-date=19 January 2021 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> Motherwell recovered to repeat their fifth place finish the following year, but scored considerably fewer goals,<ref name="fchd">{{cite web |url=https://fchd.info/MOTHERWE.HTM |title=Motherwell |website=fchd.info |access-date=4 January 2020}}</ref> Ferguson himself recording his lowest tally for the club in a full season after scoring 19 times in the league from 26 appearances, though he remained the team's top scorer.<ref name="pw"/><ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000473/19190516/082/0005 |url-access=subscription |title=The Fleeting Show |newspaper=Motherwell Times |page=5 |date=16 May 1919 |access-date=4 January 2020 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref>

Ferguson scored his first goals of the [[1919–20 in Scottish football|1919–20 season]] by netting a hat-trick in an opening-game victory over Dundee.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000473/19190822/036/0002 |url-access=subscription |title=Motherwell 3; Dundee 1 |newspaper=Motherwell Times |page=2 |date=22 August 1919 |access-date=4 January 2020 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> He scored a second hat-trick against [[Hamilton Academical F.C.|Hamilton Academical]] three weeks later, for which he received a gold watch from local businessman T. B. Hill.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000473/19190919/108/0007 |url-access=subscription |title=Football Notes |newspaper=Motherwell Times |page=7 |date=19 September 1919 |access-date=4 January 2020 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> Ferguson followed this up with a [[Brace (sports)|brace]] against Raith Rovers in Motherwell's next game, placing him as the Scottish League's top goalscorer, having netted eleven times in his first six appearances, three ahead of his nearest rival.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000540/19190915/255/0008 |url-access=subscription |title=Celtic Victory at Tynecastle |newspaper=The Scotsman |page=8 |date=15 September 1919 |access-date=4 January 2020 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> Ferguson also scored in all of his league appearances until 6 October, when his side were beaten by Dundee.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000563/19191008/137/0011 |url-access=subscription |title=Narrow Escape of Rangers' Player |newspaper=Dundee Evening Telegraph |page=11 |date=8 October 1919 |access-date=4 January 2020 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> His early season form led to his selection in a Scottish League XI to face an Irish League XI,<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000576/19191022/092/0006 |url-access=subscription |title=Scottish Team Against Ireland |newspaper=Aberdeen Press and Journal |page=6 |date=22 October 1919 |access-date=4 January 2020 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> but he withdrew from the match after sustaining an injury in a league match against [[Heart of Midlothian F.C.|Heart of Midlothian]] the week before.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000511/19191104/021/0005 |url-access=subscription |title=Change in Scottish Team |newspaper=Exeter and Plymouth Gazette |page=5 |date=4 November 1919 |access-date=4 January 2020 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> The injury kept Ferguson out for five weeks before making his return against [[Partick Thistle F.C.|Partick Thistle]] in mid-December.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000563/19191216/098/0011 |url-access=subscription |title=No Prizes for Goalscorers |newspaper=Dundee Evening Telegraph |page=11 |date=16 December 1919 |access-date=4 January 2020 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref>

Despite his absence, by the end of January 1920, Ferguson remained tied with [[James Williamson (footballer)|James Williamson]] of [[Hibernian F.C.|Hibernian]] as the league's top goalscorer with 21.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000452/19200131/087/0008 |url-access=subscription |title=Sport of All Sorts |newspaper=Edinburgh Evening News |page=8 |date=31 January 1920 |access-date=4 January 2020 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> Ferguson moved ahead of his rival after scoring a hat-trick during a 5–1 win over [[Clyde F.C.|Clyde]] in early February.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000540/19200216/047/0010 |url-access=subscription |title=Scottish League |newspaper=The Scotsman |page=10 |date=16 February 1920 |access-date=4 January 2020 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> He was selected for the Scottish League XI for a second time and played in a 4–0 defeat to their English league counterparts on 20 March. His performance was criticised in ''The Sunday Post'' after he missed several chances to score.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000566/19200321/074/0013 |url-access=subscription |title=Fleet – footed Englishmen Show Up The Scots |newspaper=Sunday Post |page=13 |date=21 March 1920 |access-date=4 January 2020 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> Ferguson finished the season as the Scottish League's top goalscorer with 33.<ref name="ts">{{cite web |url=https://www.rsssf.org/tabless/scottops.html |title= Scotland – List of Topscorers |website=[[RSSSF]] |access-date=4 January 2020}}</ref> His goals helped Motherwell claim third position in the league, their highest finish in the top tier at the time.<ref name="fchd"/>

====Record-breaking season====

Ferguson continued his good form into the [[1920–21 in Scottish football|1920–21 season]] and netted four of his side's goals during a 6–0 win over Queen's Park early in the campaign.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000540/19200920/316/0008 |url-access=subscription |title=The Rangers Suffer Defeat |newspaper=The Scotsman |page=8 |date=20 September 1920 |access-date=4 January 2020 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> He followed this up with a further hat-trick against [[Falkirk F.C.|Falkirk]] two weeks later and ended October as the joint-highest scorer in the league.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000566/19200926/089/0013 |url-access=subscription |title=Ferguson's Hat Trick |newspaper=Sunday Post |page=13 |date=26 September 1920 |access-date=4 January 2020 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0001464/19201026/160/0006 |url-access=subscription |title=The World of Sport |newspaper=Sheffield Independent |page=6 |date=26 October 1920 |access-date=4 January 2020 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> His goalscoring form attracted attention from clubs in England's [[English Football League|Football League]] and both [[Everton F.C.|Everton]] and [[Huddersfield Town A.F.C.|Huddersfield Town]] made enquiries to Motherwell over a transfer but were turned down.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000566/19201031/069/0014 |url-access=subscription |title=Football |newspaper=Sunday Post |page=14 |date=31 October 1920 |access-date=4 January 2020 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0001914/19210319/013/0001 |url-access=subscription |title=Still Searching |newspaper=Star Green 'Un |page=1 |date=19 March 1921 |access-date=4 January 2020 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> He scored a further four goals during an 8–2 victory over bottom-placed side Dumbarton in November, reaching 21 goals for the season and becoming the highest-scoring player in Scotland and England at the time.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000540/19201122/214/0004 |url-access=subscription |title=Ten Goals at Motherwell |newspaper=The Scotsman |page=4 |date=22 November 1920 |access-date=4 January 2020 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0002710/19201124/101/0004 |url-access=subscription |title=Stud Marks |newspaper=Coatbridge Express |page=4 |date=24 November 1920 |access-date=4 January 2020 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref>

One month later, Ferguson scored four goals in a single game for the third time during the season, this time in a 6–1 win over Ayr United.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000473/19201231/039/0002 |url-access=subscription |title=Motherwell 6; Ayr United 1 |newspaper=Motherwell Times |page=2 |date=31 December 1920 |access-date=4 January 2020 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> On 16 April, he repeated his four-goal achievement for the fourth time during the season in a victory over [[Aberdeen F.C.|Aberdeen]]. In doing so, he surpassed the Scottish League record for goals in a single season of 39 set by Rangers' [[Willie Reid (footballer, born 1884)|Willie Reid]] before the First World War. Ferguson's four goals took his tally to 40 for the season.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000563/19210418/124/0011 |url-access=subscription |title=Ferguson of Motherwell's Goal Scoring |newspaper=Dundee Evening Telegraph |page=11 |date=18 April 1911 |access-date=4 January 2020 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> He ended the campaign with 42 goals to finish as the league's top goalscorer for the third time in four seasons. His goals record would last until the following year, when [[Duncan Walker]] scored 45 goals for St Mirren.<ref name="ts"/>

During the opening months of the [[1921–22 in Scottish football|1921–22 season]], Ferguson scored his first hat-trick of the campaign against Dumbarton,<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000566/19211016/090/0013 |url-access=subscription |title=Motherwell's Five |newspaper=Sunday Post |page=13 |date=16 October 1921 |access-date=20 January 2021 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> by which time he was one goal short of Walker.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000473/19211021/117/0007 |url-access=subscription |title=Notes and Pars |newspaper=Motherwell Times |page=7 |date=21 October 1921 |access-date=20 January 2021 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> Two weeks later, he received his second cap for the Scottish League XI in October and scored for the side as they defeated their Irish counterparts 3–0.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0001542/19211027/067/0003 |url-access=subscription |title=Inter-League Match |newspaper=Northern Whig |page=3 |date=27 October 1921 |access-date=20 January 2021 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> In December, he scored all of Motherwell's goals in a 5–2 victory over [[Clydebank F.C. (1914)|Clydebank]], four of which came in the first half.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000566/19211211/072/0012 |url-access=subscription |title=Five for Ferguson at Motherwell |newspaper=Sunday Post |page=12 |date=12 December 1921 |access-date=20 January 2021 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> Ferguson was frequently linked with moves to English clubs, but the ''Sunday Post'' reported in January 1922 that he had no desire to move out of Scotland and did not enjoy the English style of football.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000566/19220101/060/0012 |url-access=subscription |title=Hugh Ferguson Not Going to England |newspaper=Sunday Post |page=12 |date=1 January 1922 |access-date=20 January 2021 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> Ferguson fell ten short of Walker in the latter's record breaking season, finishing as the second-highest scorer in the league with 35.<ref name="pw"/>

====Near transfer and later years====

At the end of the 1921–22 season Ferguson was placed on the transfer list by Motherwell at his own behest;<ref name="tl">{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000164/19220502/085/0006 |url-access=subscription |title=Hugh Ferguson on Transfer List |newspaper=Dundee Courier |page=6 |date=2 May 1922 |access-date=5 January 2020 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000566/19220430/094/0014 |url-access=subscription |title=Hugh Ferguson's Future |newspaper=Sunday Post |page=14 |date=30 April 1922 |access-date=21 January 2021 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> this prompted approaches from several clubs.<ref name="tl"/> In June 1922, Manchester City submitted an offer of £3,500 ({{Inflation|UK|3500|1922|fmt=eq|cursign=£|r=-4}}{{Inflation/fn|UK}}) which was rejected by Motherwell, who valued Ferguson at around £4,000 (£{{Inflation|UK|4000|1922|fmt=c|r=-4}}{{Inflation/fn|UK}}). City returned with a bid of £3,900 (£{{Inflation|UK|3900|1922|fmt=c|r=-4}}{{Inflation/fn|UK}}) which was accepted by the Motherwell board but the transfer collapsed when Ferguson turned down the move.<ref>{{Cite web |url=http://www.motherwellfc.co.uk/the-club/history/club-history/1918-1929/ |title=1918–1929 |publisher=Motherwell F.C. |access-date=28 April 2016 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180329060214/http://www.motherwellfc.co.uk/the-club/history/club-history/1918-1929/ |archive-date=29 March 2018}}</ref> He also held talks with [[Football League Third Division South]] side [[AFC Bournemouth|Bournemouth & Boscombe Athletic]] over a role as [[player-manager]],<ref name="truth">{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000566/19220827/074/0013 |url-access=subscription |title=The Truth About Motherwell's Centre |newspaper=Sunday Post |page=13 |date=27 August 1922 |access-date=5 January 2020 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> which was reported by some newspapers as being completed.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000164/19220825/112/0006 |url-access=subscription |title=Hugh Ferguson Goes South |newspaper=Dundee Courier |page=6 |date=25 August 1922 |access-date=5 January 2020 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> Ultimately no move came to fruition; Ferguson considered it unfair that he should receive no part of a potential transfer fee and he sat out the early weeks of the [[1922–23 in Scottish football|1922–23 season]].<ref name="truth"/> He eventually re-signed for Motherwell and made his return to the team in a 2–1 defeat against Aberdeen on 2 September, scoring his side's goal.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000566/19220903/078/0012 |url-access=subscription |title=Ferguson Enhances His Reputation |newspaper=Sunday Post |page=12 |date=3 September 1922 |access-date=5 January 2020 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref>

Having scored six times by mid-October, Ferguson was called up to the Scottish League XI for a match against Ireland when [[George French (Scottish footballer)|George French]] withdrew due to injury.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000164/19221017/113/0006 |url-access=subscription |title=Football Facts and Fancies |newspaper=Dundee Courier |page=6 |date=17 October 1922 |access-date=21 January 2021 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0002318/19221002/039/0002 |url-access=subscription |title=Gossip of the Clubs |newspaper=Belfast Telegraph |page=2 |date=2 October 1922 |access-date=21 January 2021 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> He scored a brace for the side as they won the match 3–0.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000164/19221019/139/0006 |url-access=subscription |title=No Match for the Scots |newspaper=Dundee Courier |page=6 |date=19 October 1922 |access-date=21 January 2021 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> Hat-tricks for Motherwell against [[Kilmarnock F.C.|Kilmarnock]] and Clyde in November doubled his tally for the season to 12 by the end of the month.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000164/19221106/095/0006 |url-access=subscription |title=From Field and Pavilion |newspaper=Dunde Courier |page=6 |date=6 November 1922 |access-date=21 January 2021 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000164/19221128/137/0006 |url-access=subscription |title=Latest Football Gossip |newspaper=Dundee Courier |page=6 |date=28 November 1922 |access-date=21 January 2021 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000566/19221126/062/0012 |url-access=subscription |title=Yesterday's Tit-bits |newspaper=Sunday Post |page=12 |date=26 November 1922 |access-date=21 January 2021 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> Ferguson continued his streak of hat-tricks, adding further instances in December, January and February to take his tally of hat-tricks to five for the season.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000164/19230104/114/0006 |url-access=subscription |title=Good Capture by East Fife |newspaper=Dundee Courier |page=6 |date=4 January 1923 |access-date=21 January 2021 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000473/19230216/119/0007 |url-access=subscription |title=Football Notes |newspaper=Motherwell Times |page=7 |date=16 February 1923 |access-date=21 January 2021 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> He also netted hat-tricks in victories over Falkirk and [[Bo'ness F.C.|Bo'ness]] in the [[Scottish Cup]].<ref>{{cite news |date=11 March 1923 |title=How Celts Made Siccar at Ibrox |page=12 |newspaper=Sunday Post |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000566/19230311/135/0012 |url-access=subscription |access-date=22 January 2021 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> The latter victory in the fourth round resulted in Motherwell reaching the semi-final of the competition for the first time in the club's history.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000566/19230225/126/0012 |url-access=subscription |title=Three for Hugh Ferguson |newspaper=Sunday Post |page=12 |date=25 February 1923 |access-date=21 January 2021 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> Ferguson finished the season as the second-highest scorer in Scotland with 29 league goals, despite missing 9 games throughout the campaign,<ref name="pw"/> falling one short of Hearts' [[Jock White]].<ref name="ts"/>

[[File:Third lanark 1923.jpg|thumb|[[Third Lanark A.C.|Third Lanark]]'s team during their 1923 tour – Ferguson standing, third from left]]
In the summer of 1923, Third Lanark arranged a tour of South America,<ref name=badbhoy>{{cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1xojAwAAQBAJ&q=%22willie+frame%22+motherwell&pg=PT52 |title=Tommy McInally: Celtic's Bad Bhoy |last=Potter |first=David |publisher=Black & White Publishing |location=Edinburgh|year=2009 |isbn=9781845025786}}</ref> but found themselves short of players. The team subsequently invited players from other Scottish teams to travel, with Ferguson agreeing to take part.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000986/19230514/165/0008 |url-access=subscription |title=Are Third Lanark Wise? |newspaper=Athletic News |page=8 |date=14 May 1923 |access-date=22 January 2021 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref><ref name="bbc.co.uk">{{cite web |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-scotland-65890166 |title=Third Lanark's lasting legacy on South American football |website=BBC News |date=18 June 2023 |accessdate=18 June 2023}}</ref> He scored eight goals in as many matches against opponents including [[CA Independiente|Independiente]], [[Peñarol]] and a [[Uruguay national football team|Uruguay XI]].<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.rsssf.org/tables/3rdlanark-satrip23.html |title=Río de la Plata Trip of Third Lanark 1923 |last=Ciullini |first=Pablo |website=[[RSSSF]] |access-date=19 August 2020}}</ref> Ahead of the tour, the ''[[Athletic News]]'' wrote that Ferguson had been "the most effective centre-forward in Great Britain" during the early 1920s.<ref name="an2">{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000986/19230625/072/0004 |url-access=subscription |title=H. Ferguson's Record |newspaper=Athletic News |page=4 |date=24 June 1923 |access-date=5 January 2020 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> During the following season, he was again among the top scorers early in the year and, by the end of October, had scored 12 goals leaving him trailing only [[Tom Jennings (footballer)|Tom Jennings]] of Raith Rovers.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0001907/19231026/092/0007 |url-access=subscription |title=Football Notes |newspaper=Linlithgowshire Gazette |page=7 |date=26 October 1923 |access-date=23 January 2021 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> Two weeks later, Ferguson was sent off in a 2–1 victory over Queen's Park after an altercation with an opposing defender when he retaliated against some rough play.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000473/19231123/128/0007 |url-access=subscription |title=Motherwell 2; Queen's Park 1 |newspaper=Motherwell Times |page=7 |date=23 November 1923 |access-date=23 January 2021 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> He later received a reprimand from the [[Scottish Football Association]] but avoided any further punishment.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000164/19231213/109/0006 |url-access=subscription |title=Hugh Ferguson Censured |newspaper=Dundee Courier |page=6 |date=13 December 1923 |access-date=23 January 2021 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> Ferguson finished third in the scorers' ranking, behind Dundee forward [[Dave Halliday]] and Jennings, registering 27 goals in 33 league appearances.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000986/19240505/002/0001 |url-access=subscription |title=Great Goal-getting |newspaper=Athletic News |page=1 |date=5 May 1924 |access-date=5 January 2020 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref>

Despite his prolific scoring record, at the beginning of the [[1924–25 in Scottish football|1924–25 season]], Motherwell experimented by moving Ferguson to inside right rather than his usual central position.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0001914/19240906/008/0001 |url-access=subscription |title=Punts, Points and Passes |newspaper=Star Green 'Un |page=1 |date=6 September 1924 |access-date=23 January 2021 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> Although he frequented both positions, Ferguson maintained his scoring consistency and was one goal off being the league's highest goalscorer by late October.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000563/19241020/107/0011 |url-access=subscription |title=Hat-trick brings Fergie Up |newspaper=Dundee Evening Telegraph |page=11 |date=20 October 1924 |access-date=23 January 2021 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> In January 1925, Ferguson was approached to play in the United States, but refused the offer.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0001907/19250109/094/0005 |url-access=subscription |title=Clean Food |newspaper=Linlithgow Gazette |page=7 |date=9 January 1925 |access-date=24 January 2021 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> Later the same month, he scored five times in a 6–3 win against [[Galston F.C.|Galston]] in the first round of the Scottish Cup.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000566/19250125/053/0012 |url-access=subscription |title=Five for Hugh Ferguson |newspaper=Sunday Post |page=12 |date=25 January 1925 |access-date=24 January 2021 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> Motherwell finished the season in 18th place, their lowest ranking since before the First World War.<ref name="fchd"/> Ferguson however was the joint fourth-highest scorer in the league,<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000473/19250501/091/0005 |url-access=subscription |title=How the World Wags |newspaper=Motherwell Times |page=5 |date=1 May 1925 |access-date=25 January 2021 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> but remained on the transfer list as the club looked to raise funds.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000566/19250531/111/0012 |url-access=subscription |title=New Player for Falkirk |newspaper=Sunday Post |page=12 |date=31 May 1925 |access-date=25 January 2021 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref>
 
Despite his listing, Ferguson remained with Motherwell for the start of the [[1925–26 in Scottish football|1925–26 season]] and began the campaign by scoring a brace in a victory over Clydebank on the opening day.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000566/19250816/089/0012 |url-access=subscription |title=Hugh Ferguson Gets Two |newspaper=Sunday Post |page=12 |date=16 August 1925 |access-date=25 January 2021 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> In his final season with the club, Ferguson scored 12 times in 12 appearances,<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0001084/19251113/698/0028 |url-access=subscription |title=Hugh Ferguson Goes to Cardiff |newspaper=Derbyshire Advertiser and Journal |page=8 |date=8 November 1925 |access-date=5 January 2020 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> leaving him one goal behind [[David McCrae]] as the second-highest scoring player in the Scottish league at the time.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0001062/19251031/211/0010 |url-access=subscription |title=Snap Shots |newspaper=Fife Free Press |page=10 |date=31 October 1925 |access-date=25 January 2021 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> His last goals for the club came in a 2–0 win over Hamilton in which he scored both of his side's goals.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000164/19251020/092/0006 |url-access=subscription |title=Hobbs of Football |newspaper=Dundee Courier |page=6 |date=20 October 1925 |access-date=25 January 2021 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000264/19251019/013/0004 |url-access=subscription |title=Bristol City Win a Point |newspaper=Western Daily Press |page=4 |date=19 October 1925 |access-date=25 January 2021 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref>

===Cardiff City===
In November 1925, officials from Welsh side [[Cardiff City F.C.|Cardiff City]] submitted an offer for Ferguson. Their bid was rejected but they returned to meet Motherwell's asking price soon afterwards;<ref name="mtfee"/> Ferguson signed for a fee of £5,000 ({{Inflation|UK|5000|1925|fmt=eq|cursign=£|r=-4}}),{{Inflation/fn|UK}} just £1,000 less than the [[Progression of the British football transfer fee record|record transfer fee]] at the time.<ref name="Cup friends reunited">{{Cite news |url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/football/7328650.stm |title=Cup friends reunited |publisher=BBC |work=[[BBC Sport]] |last=Shuttleworth |first=Peter |date=4 January 2010 |access-date=28 August 2010}}</ref> Such was his popularity at the Scottish club that the local steelworks closed for half an hour as the workers lined the streets to wave Ferguson off.<ref name="Tragic Scots FA Cup hero"/> A large crowd also gathered at the train station as he departed and a salute of 21 [[Foghorn|fog signals]] was issued from the [[David Colville & Sons]] works as he passed.<ref name="mtfee"/> Cardiff manager [[Fred Stewart (football manager)|Fred Stewart]], who was accompanying him, remarked that "he had never seen a player given such a send-off before".<ref name="so">{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000104/19251106/168/0006 |url-access=subscription |title=Cardiff City's Team |newspaper=Western Mail |page=6 |date=6 November 1925 |access-date=26 January 2021 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> Ferguson was one of three Scots signed by Cardiff in short succession, [[Joe Cassidy (footballer, born 1896)|Joe Cassidy]] and [[George McLachlan]] both arriving for a similar £5,000 fee combined.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000327/19251114/012/0004 |url-access=subscription |title=The Whirligig of Football |newspaper=Derby Daily Telegraph |page=4 |date=14 November 1925 |access-date=5 January 2020 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> Ferguson made a goalscoring debut for the club in the [[Football League First Division]]{{refn|group=nb|Despite being based in Wales, Cardiff City compete in the [[English football league system]].}} on 7 November 1925 in a 5–2 win over [[Leicester City F.C.|Leicester City]],<ref name="Hayes">{{Cite book | title = The Who's Who of Cardiff City | first = Dean | last = Hayes | publisher = Breedon Books | year = 2006 | page=63 | location=Derby | isbn= 1-85983-462-0}}</ref> a match in which Cassidy scored a hat-trick.<ref name="S27">{{harvnb|Shepherd|2002|p=27}}</ref>

After scoring once in his first three appearances, Ferguson embarked on a goalscoring run. He netted seven times in his next five games, including the winning goals in matches against [[Bolton Wanderers F.C.|Bolton Wanderers]], [[Notts County F.C.|Notts County]] and [[West Bromwich Albion F.C.|West Bromwich Albion]]. In his first season, Ferguson finished as the club's top goalscorer with 19 league goals, despite having played in only half of Cardiff's matches. His tally also included his first hat-trick in the Football League during a 4–2 victory over Notts County in April 1926. Cardiff ended the campaign in 16th place, avoiding [[Promotion and relegation|relegation]].<ref name="S27"/> The improvement in the team's attacking prowess following their new signings was credited for the improved form during the latter half of the season, Ferguson's goals being a cited as a major factor.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000327/19260410/018/0004 |url-access=subscription |title=Safe and Sound |newspaper=Derby Daily Telegraph |page=4 |date=10 April 1926 |access-date=26 January 2021 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref>

He was again the club's top goalscorer in the [[1926–27 Cardiff City F.C. season|1926–27 season]], netting 26 times in 39 league games,<ref name="S28">{{harvnb|Shepherd|2002|p=28}}</ref> despite often playing at inside right as the club looked to fit both Ferguson and [[Len Davies]] into the starting lineup.<ref name="herald">{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000681/19270412/130/0008 |url-access=subscription |title=Hugh Ferguson |newspaper=Daily Herald |page=8 |date=12 April 1927 |access-date=5 January 2020 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> During the campaign, Ferguson was instrumental in Cardiff's progression to the FA Cup final. He scored twice in their first four fixtures before netting the winning goal in a fourth round [[Replay (sports)|replay]] against [[Chelsea F.C.|Chelsea]] by converting a penalty kick. Ahead of their fourth-round tie with reigning Cup holders Bolton Wanderers, Ferguson adopted a "lucky" black cat named Trixie that he had noticed wandering at the [[Royal Birkdale Golf Club]] in [[Southport]], where the Cardiff team were staying. The cat had been following the players and it was remarked that it "showed distinct partiality for Hughie".<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000473/19270422/148/0008 |url-access=subscription |title=Hugh Ferguson's Black Cat |newspaper=Motherwell Times |page=8 |date=22 April 1927 |access-date=16 January 2021 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> Ferguson tracked down the animal's owners and persuaded them to give the cat away in exchange for tickets to the final if Cardiff advanced.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.cardiffcityfc.co.uk/news/2017/april/90-years---a-lucky-black-cat |title=90 Years – A Lucky Cat |publisher=Cardiff City F.C. |access-date=15 January 2021}}</ref> In the semi-final, he scored a brace against [[Reading F.C.|Reading]] as Cardiff won 3–0 to reach the [[1927 FA Cup Final]].<ref name="S28"/>

Having featured at inside right in all rounds leading up to the final,<ref name="herald"/> Ferguson was switched to centre-forward for the deciding match.<ref name="S28"/> In the 74th minute of the game, collecting a throw from the right, Ferguson hurried a tame shot toward the Arsenal goal. [[Dan Lewis (footballer)|Dan Lewis]], the Arsenal goalkeeper, appeared to collect the ball but, under pressure from the advancing Len Davies, allowed the ball to roll through his grasp; in a desperate attempt to retrieve the ball, Lewis only succeeded in knocking the ball with his elbow into his own net.<ref name="yorkshirefinal">{{cite news|title=The Association Cup|url=http://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000687/19270425/149/0003|access-date=6 April 2016|work=Yorkshire Post and Leeds Intelligencer|issue=24894|date=25 April 1927|page=3|url-access=subscription |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> [[Ernie Curtis]], the 19‑year‑old teammate of Ferguson, said of the goal:

:"''I was in line with the edge of the [[penalty area]] on the right when Hughie Ferguson hit the shot which Arsenal's goalie had crouched down for a little early. The ball spun as it travelled towards him, having taken a slight deflection so he was now slightly out of line with it. Len Davies was following the shot in and I think Dan must have had one eye on him. The result was that he didn't take it cleanly and it squirmed under him and over the line. Len jumped over him and into the net, but never actually touched it.''"<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.espn.com/soccer/columns/story/_/id/1024815/rewind-to-1927:-cardiff-claim-cup-glory |title=Cardiff Claim Cup Glory |publisher=ESPN |last=Carter |first=Jon |date=12 February 2012 |access-date=5 January 2020}}</ref>

Ferguson's goal led Cardiff to become the only team from outside England to have won the competition as they went on to win the match 1–0.<ref name="Cup friends reunited"/> His 26 league goals and 6 in the team's [[1926–27 FA Cup|FA Cup]] run set a new club record for goals in a season with 32. The record stood until 2003 when [[Robert Earnshaw]] scored 35 in a single season.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/football/teams/c/cardiff_city/2855393.stm |title=Ernie Sends Records Tumbling |publisher=BBC Sport |date=22 March 2003 |access-date=6 January 2020}}</ref> The following season, Ferguson netted seven times in his first ten league matches and also scored in a 2–1 victory over amateur side [[Corinthian F.C.|Corinthians]] in the [[1927 FA Charity Shield]] on 12 October. Cardiff finished the season in sixth place but Ferguson's later months were hampered by injuries; he never played more than three consecutive league matches after the Christmas period. Despite this, he was again Cardiff's top goalscorer, netting 18 times in the league. In April 1928, he scored both goals in the final of the [[Welsh Cup]] as Cardiff defeated [[Bangor City F.C.|Bangor]] 2–0.<ref name="S29">{{harvnb|Shepherd|2002|p=29}}</ref>

Ferguson scored a penalty on the opening day of the [[1928–29 Cardiff City F.C. season|1928–29 season]] to give his side a 1–1 draw against [[Newcastle United F.C.|Newcastle United]]. In the team's second match, he set a club record for goals in a league game after scoring five times against [[Burnley F.C.|Burnley]] on 1 September 1928 as Cardiff ran out 7–0 winners.<ref name="S30">{{harvnb|Shepherd|2002|p=30}}</ref> After eight matches, he led the First Division as its top goalscorer with ten goals.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0002751/19280928/083/0003 |url-access=subscription |title=Ferguson's Ten |newspaper=Athletic Montrose Standard |page=3 |date=28 September 1928 |access-date=6 January 2020 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> His scoring saw him surpass [[Steve Bloomer]] as the highest-scoring player in Scottish and English football on 352 goals, doing so in less time.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000473/19281123/123/0007 |url-access=subscription |title=Hugh Ferguson's Wonderful Record |newspaper=Motherwell Times |page=7 |date=23 November 1928 |access-date=7 January 2020 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> He then began to suffer from persistent injury problems and, after playing in a 1–0 defeat to [[Aston Villa F.C.|Aston Villa]] at the end of September, he made only three further appearances before Christmas.<ref name="S30" /><ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0001464/19281024/278/0010 |url-access=subscription |title=Cardiff City's Visit to Hillsborough |newspaper=Sheffield Independent |page=10 |date=24 October 1928 |access-date=7 January 2020 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> This coincided with a drop in form for Cardiff, who scored only 7 times in their next 13 games,<ref name="S30" /> during which the ''[[Western Mail (Wales)|Western Mail]]'' bemoaned Ferguson's absence.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000104/19281022/100/0004 |url-access=subscription |title=Sunderland's Polished Play |newspaper=Western Mail |page=4 |date=22 October 1928 |access-date=30 January 2021 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> He made his final league appearance for Cardiff on 23 February 1929, scoring in a 2–2 draw against [[Manchester United F.C.|Manchester United]].<ref name="S30" /> With Cardiff struggling to avoid relegation and suffering financial problems, Ferguson was named as one of 12 players for whom the club was looking to obtain offers.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000640/19290312/251/0009 |url-access=subscription |title=Sports Review |newspaper=Sunderland Daily Echo and Shipping Gazette |page=9 |date=12 March 1929 |access-date=7 January 2020 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> His position was further weakened by the signing of another centre-forward, [[Jimmy Munro (footballer, born 1905)|Jimmy Munro]].<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000564/19290305/182/0009 |url-access=subscription |title=Centre for Transfer |newspaper=Dundee Courier |page=9 |date=5 March 1929 |access-date=30 January 2021 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> Despite playing only 20 of the club's 42 league matches, Ferguson finished as Cardiff's top goalscorer for the fourth consecutive season with 14 league goals. This was also his 14th consecutive season as the top goalscorer of his team, at both Motherwell and Cardiff.<ref name="dp"/> His last appearance for the club came on 1 May, when he played in a 3–0 defeat to [[Connah's Quay & Shotton F.C.|Connah's Quay & Shotton]] in the Welsh Cup final.<ref name="S30" />

===Dundee===
Ferguson returned to Scotland with Dundee in June 1929. Cardiff had hoped to recoup £1,000 ({{Inflation|UK|1000|1929|fmt=eq|cursign=£|r=-4}}{{Inflation/fn|UK}}) for him but the transfer was eventually completed for around £800 (£{{Inflation|UK|800|1925|fmt=c|r=-4}}{{Inflation/fn|UK}}).<ref name="dp">{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000564/19290615/113/0007 |url-access=subscription |title=From Cardiff to Dens Park |newspaper=Dundee Courier |page=7 |date=15 June 1929 |access-date=7 January 2020 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> Ferguson had openly expressed his preference to return to Motherwell, an option that the club considered, but the move never came to fruition.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000473/19290614/158/0008 |url-access=subscription |title=Dundee and Hughie |newspaper=Motherwell Times |page=8 |date=14 June 1929 |access-date=30 January 2021 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> He made his debut for Dundee on the opening day of the season against Falkirk,<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000564/19290812/118/0008 |url-access=subscription |title=Dundee's Joy Day at Firhill |newspaper=Dundee Courier |page=8 |date=12 August 1929 |access-date=30 January 2021 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> and began the campaign at centre-forward but struggled to live up to his goalscoring reputation. His first goal for the club came in October, when he netted the only goal of the game against Queen's Park.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000986/19291007/118/0015 |url-access=subscription |title=Amateurs' Hard Fight |newspaper=Athletic News |page=15 |date=7 October 1929 |access-date=30 January 2021 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> The ''[[The Courier (Dundee)|Dundee Courier]]'' later reported that he was "not enjoying the best of health" at the time but played on. He was later moved to outside right before being dropped from the side entirely. His final game for the club was a 3–0 victory over Heart of Midlothian on 14 December 1929.<ref name="Tragic Scots FA Cup hero"/><ref name="passing">{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000564/19300109/150/0008 |url-access=subscription |title=Passing of Hugh Ferguson |newspaper=Dundee Courier |page=8 |last=John |first=Don |date=9 January 1930 |access-date=7 January 2020 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> His performance in the match was criticised and he was subsequently dropped from the first team.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000563/19291220/142/0013 |url-access=subscription |title=Dundee's Right Wing Change |newspaper=Dundee Evening Telegraph |page=13 |date=20 December 1929 |access-date=30 January 2021 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> It was later reported that he had been suffering severe cramp in his legs that had left him struggling to sprint.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000563/19291217/154/0009 |url-access=subscription |title=Ferguson Cramped |newspaper=Dundee Evening Telegraph |page=9 |date=17 December 1929 |access-date=30 January 2021 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref>

==Death==
Ferguson sank into [[Mental health in association football|depression]] and it was remarked that he displayed a "melancholy demeanour and evident physical suffering" in early January 1930 by his former colleagues at Motherwell.<ref name="passing"/> He had been suffering from insomnia for several months at the end of 1929 and had become worried about his health.<ref name="FF"/> On 8 January, he committed [[suicide]], [[Poison gas|gassing]] himself at Dundee's ground [[Dens Park]] after remaining behind following a training session. His body was discovered the next morning by painters working at the ground. He was next to a gas ring which had been turned on, and his head was covered by an overcoat. He was rushed to [[Dundee Royal Infirmary]] but was pronounced dead.<ref name="df">{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000564/19300109/024/0003 |url-access=subscription |title=Death of Dundee Footballer |newspaper=Dundee Courier |page=3 |date=9 January 1930 |access-date=7 January 2020 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> His death has often been cited as being influenced by the barracking of the crowd as he failed to produce the form expected of him on his return to Scotland.<ref name="ind">{{cite news |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/sport/football/fa-league-cups/the-tragedy-of-hughie-ferguson-829972.html |title=The Tragedy of Hughie Ferguson |newspaper=The Independent |last=Corrigan |first=James |date=23 October 2011 |access-date=7 January 2020}}</ref> In his last game for Dundee, he received considerable criticism from the crowd as he struggled with cramp. The ''Dundee Evening Telegraph'' remarked that, for Ferguson, "the experience of that afternoon was tremendously painful."<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000563/19300108/001/0001 |url-access=subscription |title=Dundee Footballer's Tragic Death |newspaper=Dundee Evening Telegraph |page=1 |date=8 January 1930 |access-date=31 January 2021 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> William McIntosh, a director of Dundee, commented that "(Ferguson) did his best for Dundee and he took his failure to heart. His lack of success was certainly not due to lack of trying {{nbsp}}... lately he became obsessed with the idea that his usefulness as a footballer had come to an end."<ref name="df"/>

Aged 34, Ferguson left behind his wife Jessie and two children.<ref name="Cup friends reunited"/> Jessie was pregnant with the couple's third child at the time.<ref name="ind"/> His family have attributed his death to "an imbalance of his inner-ear" that had affected his balance and had led to his poor form. They believe this was caused by an undiagnosed brain tumour.<ref name="Tragic Scots FA Cup hero"/><ref name="ind"/> Ferguson's body was moved from Dundee to Motherwell; four of his Dundee teammates carried his coffin to a waiting hearse while the remainder of the squad watched on. A small service was held at his home before he was buried in [[Airbles]] Cemetery in his hometown of Motherwell.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000564/19300111/043/0003 |url-access=subscription |title=Funeral of Dundee Footballer |newspaper=Dundee Courier |page=3 |date=11 January 1930 |access-date=7 January 2020 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref>

==Family and personal life==
Ferguson was born in Motherwell on 2 March 1895 and had six brothers and one sister.<ref name="Hayes"/><ref name="FF"/><ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000473/19300117/032/0002 |url-access=subscription |title=Tributes at Footballer's Funeral |newspaper=Motherwell Times |page=2 |date=17 January 1930 |access-date=16 January 2021 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> His father was killed in a [[Coal mining|colliery]] accident when Ferguson was 16.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000104/19300110/277/0012 |url-access=subscription |title=The Tragedy of Ferguson |newspaper=Western Mail |page=12 |date=10 January 1930 |access-date=31 January 2021 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> He married Jessie Andrews on 21 March 1924 in his hometown.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000473/19240321/015/0005 |url-access=subscription |title=The Fleeting Show |newspaper=Motherwell Times |page=5 |date=21 March 1924 |access-date=23 January 2021 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> His uncle, also named [[Hugh Ferguson (politician)|Hugh Ferguson]], was a politician and served as a [[Member of Parliament (UK)|Member of Parliament]] for Motherwell and he sometimes assisted his uncle at political gatherings.<ref name="FF"/><ref name="df"/> Ferguson's son [[Jack Ferguson (water polo, born 1930)|Jack]] later went on to represent Britain in [[water polo]] at the [[1952 Summer Olympics|1952]] and [[1956 Summer Olympics|1956]] Olympics.<ref>{{Cite news |url=https://www.heraldscotland.com/ferguson-reflects-on-past-as-he-enters-university-s-hall-of-fame-1.856980 |title=Ferguson reflects on past as he enters university's Hall of Fame |publisher=The Herald |date=28 April 2007 |access-date=22 June 2015 | first=Doug | last=Gillon}}</ref> His daughter Sadie later married Dunfermline player James Hogg, brother of Scottish international [[Bobby Hogg (footballer, born 1914)|Bobby Hogg]].<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000473/19520815/106/0007 |url-access=subscription |title=Well Known Sporting Families United at Motherwell Wedding |newspaper=Motherwell Times |page=7 |date=15 August 1952 |access-date=3 May 2021 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref>

Ferguson was regarded as a clean-living player: He was [[Teetotalism|teetotal]], never smoked and attended church.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000564/19300127/078/0004 |url-access=subscription |title=Referee's Tribute to Hugh Ferguson |newspaper=Dundee Courier |page=4 |date=27 January 1930 |access-date=31 January 2021 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> He was also interested in and kept birds, displaying them in competitions organised by the Motherwell and District Cage Bird Association. He won his category while finishing runner-up in another in 1923 with a [[British finches|goldfinch mule]] and won several awards the following year.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000473/19231116/091/0006 |url-access=subscription |title=Bird Show |newspaper=Motherwell Times |page=6 |date=16 November 1923 |access-date=23 January 2021 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref><ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000566/19241116/017/0003 |url-access=subscription |title=Latest News |newspaper=Sunday Post |page=3 |date=16 November 1924 |access-date=23 January 2021 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref>

==Style of play==
Ferguson's main attribute was his finishing ability; the ''Sporting Chronicle'' noted after his senior debut for Motherwell in 1916 that he "possessed all the physical qualities required in a centre-forward, and is unquestionably a fine marksman".<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0003159/19160823/047/0004 |url-access=subscription |title=Scottish Football Campaign |newspaper=Sporting Chronicle |page=3 |date=23 August 1916 |access-date=3 January 2020 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> At the end of his first senior season, the ''[[Daily Record (Scotland)|Daily Record]]'' wrote "Hugh can draw out the opposition, he can get a fair amount of work on the ball and he never forgets where the goal lies". The paper also referred to his physicality, noting that while it was not a major aspect of his game "he is no pigmy. He can take a knock, he can return one."<ref name="new"/> Upon his signing for Cardiff in 1925, the ''Western Mail'' described him as "not a particularly big man but he is very wiry and nippy".<ref name="so"/>

He was also renowned for his modesty and sense of fair play.<ref name="Tragic Scots FA Cup hero"/> John Hunter, who managed Ferguson at Motherwell, described him as "irreproachable in conduct, a fine upright fellow, he was above all else a gentleman."<ref name="FF">{{cite news |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0000473/19300110/092/0005 |url-access=subscription |title=Tragedy of Famous Footballer |newspaper=Motherwell Times |page=5 |date=10 January 1930 |access-date=7 January 2020 |via=British Newspaper Archive}}</ref> One such example of this came in a league match against Hibernian while playing for Motherwell. Opposition goalkeeper [[Bill Harper (footballer, born 1897)|Bill Harper]] had sustained an injury and the opportunity came to Ferguson to take advantage of this when he struggled to gather the ball from a cross. Rather than capitalise on Harper's misfortune, Ferguson allowed the keeper to recover and stop a certain goal.<ref name="FF"/>

===Scoring records===

Despite his performances for Motherwell, Ferguson was never selected for the [[Scotland national football team|Scotland national side]].<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/football/53132189 |title=Scotland: Top players never to be capped by their country |publisher=BBC Sport |last1=Elgott |first1=Jordan |last2=McPheat |first2=Nick |date=25 June 2020 |access-date=31 January 2021}}</ref> At the time of his departure from the club in 1925, Ferguson was the highest scoring player in the history of the Scottish League. He was later surpassed by [[Jimmy McGrory]] and [[Bob McPhail]].<ref name="sat">{{cite web |url=https://www.rsssf.org/tabless/scottops-allt.html |title=Scotland – All-Time Top scorers |last1=Brown |first1=Alan |last2=Robertson |first2=Forrest |last3=Ross |first3=David |website=[[RSSSF]] |access-date=5 January 2020}}</ref> He is one of only seven players to have scored more than 350 league goals in the history of Scottish and English football,<ref name="sat"/><ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.heraldscotland.com/news/12464252.81-years-on-scottish-family-still-has-pride-in-the-cardiff-jersey/ |title=81 years on, Scottish family still has pride in the Cardiff jersey |newspaper=The Herald |date=17 May 2008 |access-date=31 January 2021}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.rsssf.org/tablese/engtops-allt.html |title=Scotland – All-Time Top scorers |last1=Olenev |first1=Maxim |last2=Kaufman |first2=Nelson |website=[[RSSSF]] |access-date=8 March 2021}}</ref> finishing his career with a goal ratio per game of 0.855.<ref name="Tragic Scots FA Cup hero">{{Cite news |url=https://www.scotsman.com/sport/tragic-scots-fa-cup-hero-who-took-his-own-life-2477398 |title=Tragic Scots FA Cup hero who took his own life |work=The Scotsman |date=8 March 2008 |access-date=28 August 2010}}</ref>

For many years Ferguson held the Motherwell club record for scoring goals in consecutive games, until it was broken by [[Kevin van Veen]] in 2023.<ref name="bbc.co.uk"/>
